coredata.dc:title: L-tryptophan carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for malachite green detection

coredata.dc:description: Development of a rapid and sensitive detection method for hazardous dyes attracts considerable research interest. In this work, L-Tryptophan-based Carbon dots were developed as a fluorescence sensor for the detection of Malachite green (MG). Green fluorescent L-Trp-C-dots were synthesized by a simple pyrolysis technique using L-Trp as the starting precursor. L-Trp-C-dots exhibited different quenching responses to MG, and other interfering species, consequently offering a selective strategy to detect MG. The proposed sensor shows a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.06 μM and a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 0.22 μM with in the linearity range of 0 to 60 µM concentration. Additionally, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be below 1.7 %. Furthermore, the recovery of MG from the real-time samples (green peas) was investigated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
L-Tryptophan
Malachite green
Fluorescence quenching

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Absorption spectra of L-Trp-C-dots, (B). Image of C-dots under 365 nm UV lamp, (C) Fluorescence spectra of L-Trp C-dots at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm (D) and (E) TEM images of L-Trp-C-dots (Inset show the HRTEM image),(F) FTIR Spectra of L-Trp and L-Trp derived C-dots.
(A) The Fluorescence of C-dots at different pH Values, (B) Excitation-dependent Fluorescence spectra of L-Trp-C-dots for pH 5.

(A) Fluorescence quenching of C-dots by the addition of varying concentrations of MG for pH 5, (B) Overlapping of C-dots emission and MG absorption, (C) Stern-Volmer plot obtained by the addition of MG to C-dots, (D) Wavelength shift of fluorescence quenching of C-dots by the addition of varying concentrations of Malachite green.
Selectivity of L-Trp-C-dots towards MG, among interfering dyes and metal ions (BB, GI, Ca, Zn, Mg & K.

(A) Schematic representation of detection of MG in green peas, (B) Fluorescence spectra of the L-Trp-C-dots by the addition of different concentrations of MG, (C) Fluorescence spectra of the L-Trp-C-dots in the presence of MG in green peas at various concentrations.
Schematic illustration of direct carbonization of L-Trp to C-dots and MG detection.

head.ce:title: L-tryptophan carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for malachite green detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical and Electronics Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of a rapid and sensitive detection method for hazardous dyes attracts considerable research interest. In this work, L-Tryptophan-based Carbon dots were developed as a fluorescence sensor for the detection of Malachite green (MG). Green fluorescent L-Trp-C-dots were synthesized by a simple pyrolysis technique using L-Trp as the starting precursor. L-Trp-C-dots exhibited different quenching responses to MG, and other interfering species, consequently offering a selective strategy to detect MG. The proposed sensor shows a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.06 μM and a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 0.22 μM with in the linearity range of 0 to 60 µM concentration. Additionally, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be below 1.7 %. Furthermore, the recovery of MG from the real-time samples (green peas) was investigated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) especially, biomolecule-based C-dots have generated remarkable research attention due to their extraordinary physiochemical properties and biocompatibility [1–7]. In addition, C-dots are less toxic, highly dispersive, and stable, making them desirable for multiple applications including photoelectrical conversion, catalysis, bioimaging, drug delivery, and biomedical applications [1,4,8–10]. The physicochemical properties of C-dots can be tailored by modifying the surrounding functional groups, owing to the composition of precursor materials and the reaction conditions. The starting precursor for the C-dots enables interactions with different analytes mainly depending on the functional groups present [9,11]. Simultaneously, photoluminescence properties mostly depend on the size and morphology of the C-dots [1]. Biomolecule-based C-dots were extensively investigated and reported for their highly luminescent properties and the development of fluorescence-based sensors [12]. C-dots were synthesized from different biomolecules such as carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and amino acids [4,13,14]. Amino acids, as the basic building block of protein, are potential candidates for biocompatible C-dots and the development of biosensors. However, the sensing capabilities of amino acid-based C-dots were not explored extensively [1,14,15]. In this study, L-Trp, an essential component in various biological applications [4] was explored for synthesizing C-dots and the first-time report of its MG detection properties.

MG is a triphenylmethane dye, most widely used as dye in paper, silk, leather, wool, cotton and as well as fungicide and insecticide in aquaculture [16,17]. Recently, studies have shown that MG exhibits carcinogenic and teratogenic properties which are very harmful to humans and animals due to its high toxicity [2]. Therefore, MG is banned in many countries [2] and is not approved by the US Food and Drug Administration [18]. Despite its toxicity, affordability, antibacterial properties, and commercial availability leading to illegal use of MG continues in some areas. The creation of a low-cost and reliable sensor capable of detecting MG is crucial for ensuring food and environmental safety. There are currently various techniques that have been established to detect MG including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), electrochemistry, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [19–21]. However, these methods are complex, expensive and need long time pre-treatment and specialists to operate them. Therefore, it is still highly significant to develop a sensor with good stability, selectivity, high sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, and rapid response [22].

In this work, highly fluorescent and water-soluble C-dots were prepared using a simple one-pot technique. Further, a sensor that uses fluorescence technology has been developed to detect MG. The sensitivity, selectivity, limit of detection, and pH dependence of the sensor were evaluated. Also, the sensor was investigated for real-time analysis of MG in green peas. This study provides rapid, sensitive, and selective detection of MG from real-time samples that can be used in environmental and food safety applications.

L-Tryptophan, Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Hydrochloric acid (HCl), Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), Brilliant blue (BB), and Gram’s iodine (GI) were obtained from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL). Malachite green (MG), Potassium nitrate (KNO3), and Calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2·2H2O) were acquired from Hi Media Laboratories. Zinc acetate dihydrate Zn(CH3O2)2·2H2O was purchased from Qualigens. All the chemicals used here are of analytical grade and are used without any additional purification. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiment.

The C-dots were prepared using a simple one-pot technique. In a typical reaction procedure, 0.02 g of L-Trp was taken in a glass vial and heated to 200 °C in air by using a hotplate for 35 min. The color of the substance was changed from white to brownish-black indicating the formation of L-Trp-C-dots. The prepared C-dots were then dissolved in 2 ml of DI water and kept for further characterization.

The JASCO V-730 Spectrophotometer was used for absorption measurements. The fluorescence spectra of the samples were measured using a JASCO FP 8350 Spectrophotometer for fluorescence sensing studies. Transmission Electron microscope (TEM) images were taken in JEOL JEM 2100 (LaB6) operating voltage of 200 kV. The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR, Alpha T, Bruker, Germany) analysed the element composition and functional groups of L-Trp-C-dots.

Fig. 1D & E show the HRTEM images of the L-Trp-C-dots, indicating a size distribution ranging from 4 to 8 nm. Fig. 1D further reveals the nano-clustered morphology of the C-dots. Fig. 1E indicates the fingers with a spacing of 0.21 nm confirmation of the formation of crystalline L-Trp-C-dots [14,25]. Fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm correspond to the 100 graphite lattice plane of the C-dots [4,26,27]. The FTIR measurements (Fig. 1F) were used to analyze the surface structure of L-Trp-C-dots. The variations of the FTIR spectra of L-Trp and L-Trp-C-dots were analyzed in the range of 4000 to 500 cm−1. The peaks observed at 3403 cm−1(N–H stretching in amines), 3036 cm−1 (C–H stretching in alkenes), and 1355 cm−1 (C–N stretching in aryl amines) [24,26,28] were shifted to higher wavenumbers 3405 cm−1, 3044 cm−1 and 1357 cm−1 respectively. Alongside, the peak corresponds to CH bending in alkanes (1414 cm−1) of L-Trp[28] shifts towards the lower wavenumber (1412 cm−1). These indicate the formation of C-dots in the vicinity of these functional groups. In addition, there was a significant reduction in the intensity of FTIR spectra of L-Trp-C-dots in comparison with L-Trp, indicating the decomposition of L-Trp due to pyrolysis.

The prepared L-Trp-C-dots were investigated for pH-dependent fluorescence. Samples with pH 5, 7, and 9 were obtained by adding a constant amount of 0.1 M NaOH and 0.1 M HCl to the as-prepared L-Trp-C-dots. The samples were excited at 350 nm and their fluorescence spectra were recorded. Fig. 2 A illustrates the fluorescence intensity change with varying pH, but no wavelength shift was detected with different pH [29]. Further, the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence of L-Trp-C-dots was investigated by increasing the excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 400 nm (Fig. 2B). The figure clearly shows that the fluorescence intensity initially increased with increasing excitation wavelength, reaching a maximum at 370 nm, after which it started to decrease. The emission peak was observed at a wavelength of 446.8 nm, corresponding to the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The excitation-dependent emission of C-dots may be attributed to the particle size distribution of the C-dots. Possible fluorescence mechanisms, such as the presence of emissive traps, aromatic structures, diverse surface states, and functional groups like carboxyl, amide, oxygen-rich hydroxyl groups, excitons within the carbon framework, and free zigzag sites may lead to excitation dependent emission [30–33].

Fig. 3 A shows the fluorescence quenching of L-Trp-C-dots (with pH 5) while interacting with MG, and all the measurements were taken by an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Different concentrations (1–100 µM) of aqueous solution of MG were allowed to interact with the fluorescent L-Trp-C-dots and fluorescent quenching according to the concentration of the MG was observed. While interacting with 1 to 100 µM of MG, the fluorescence intensity varied from 1162 to 606 units. The fluorescence emission spectrum of L-Trp-C-dots overlaps with the absorption spectrum of MG (Fig. 3B), this is an indication that the fluorescence quenching may be due to Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner field effect [2,34–38]. Fig. 3C shows the Stern-Volmer plot obtained from the quenching studies, the ratio (F0/F) of fluorescence without MG (F0) to various concentrations of MG (F), it shows a linear relationship (R2 = 0.9996) between F0/F and concentration of MG, in the range of 0 µM −60 µM. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated by using equations 3S/k and 10S/k respectively (where S is the standard deviation and k is the slope of the calibration curve). The values of LOD and LOQ were determined to be 0.06 μM and 0.22 μM respectively. The fluorescence quenching of C-dots by MG can be effectively modeled using the Stern-Volmer equation.
F0/F = 1 + KSV[Q]
where F0 and F are fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of the quencher. KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant and [Q] is the concentration of quencher [38]. The trend of the Stern-Volmer plot is fitted using a polynomial fitting with R2 = 0.997. Fig. 3D shows the wavelength shift of fluorescence quenching of C-dots by the addition of varying concentrations of MG. Interestingly, a redshift of wavelength was observed with increasing concentration of MG. The wavelength shift is unique for the sensing not for pH change. So the wavelength shift together with fluorescence quenching can be used as a dual strategy to detect MG. The fluorescence spectra exhibit a red shift from 445 nm to 462 nm with an increase in MG concentration from 1 to 100 µM. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the proposed sensor, calculated from triplicate measurements (n = 3), is less than 1.7 % for various concentrations (1–100 µM) of MG (Table S-1) highlighting the high precision and reproducibility.

The selectivity and anti-interference studies were performed using different dyes and metal ions. Fig. 4 shows the selectivity of MG among selected interfering species including few dyes and metal ions (BB, GI, Ca, Zn, Mg & K). L-Trp-C-dots exhibited a substantial fluorescence quenching of 344 units while interacting with 10 µM concentration of MG. At the same time, interaction with BB, GI, Ca, Zn, Mg & K leads to a slight fluorescence quenching only, confirming the sensor's selectivity towards MG among interfering species. In addition, the anti-interference studies were carried out. From Fig. S-1, it is evident that L-Trp-C-dots can detect MG, even in the presence of interfering spices. Moreover, it is clear that the co-existence of interfering species with MG does not affect the sensor performance.

To quantify the selectivity for MG, the selectivity coefficient
( K
sc
)
of MG relative to other interfering species was calculated using the equation:
K
sc
=
S
MG
S
interferents
where,
S
MG
represents the sensitivity of the sensor to MG and
S
interferents
is the sensitivity of the sensor between other interfering species [39,40]. Table S-2 highlights the sensor’s excellent selectivity for MG over various interfering species. The proposed sensor shows higher selectivity coefficient towards Ca ion. The value of Ksc = 130.7 for Ca ions reveals that the selectivity of the sensor for MG is 130.7 times higher than that for Ca ions.

The efficacy of the developed sensor in real-time applications was evaluated through the detection of MG in green peas. Green peas, sourced from a local market, were placed in a beaker containing DI and sonicated for 10 min. The resulting extract was then used for the sensing process (Fig. 5 A). Different concentrations of MG were spiked into this solution and sensing studies were carried out, Fig. 5B shows the control and Fig. 5C shows the fluorescence quenching with varying MG concentration in real-time sample, all measurements were carried out with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The recovery percentages of 10 µM and 20 µM concentrations of MG were calculated to be 106 % and 107.5 % (Table S-3), indicating the potential of L-Trp-C-dots for real time analysis of MG in green peas samples.

Blue fluorescent L-Trp-C-dots were synthesized using a simple one-pot synthesis method. The size and crystalline characteristics of the prepared L-Trp-C-dots were confirmed using HRTEM measurements and the C-dots show a size distribution between 4 to 6 nm and a fringe spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to C-dots. Further, pH and excitation wavelength dependence of the fluorescence of the L-Trp-C-dots were investigated, and found that pH has a role in the fluorescence intensity. Also, the fluorescence peak showed a wavelength shift of 17 nm while changing excitation from 320 to 400 nm. FTIR of the sample confirms the presence of functional groups after the pyrolysis. The sensing properties of L-Trp-C-dots were examined through fluorescence quenching while interacting with malachite green (MG) showing a linear range from 0 µM to 60 µM. The selectivity of L-Trp-C-dots towards MG was confirmed among the group of dyes and metal ions (BB, GI, Ca, Zn, Mg & K). Further, showed the real-time sensing capability of the proposed fluorescence-based sensor by recovering MG from green peas and it showed a recovery of 106 % and 107.5 %. The proposed sensor for MG may have a crucial role in ensuring food safety and promoting a healthy environment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the UGC startup grant (30-566/2021 (BSR)). SS acknowledge SERB for SERB-Start up research grant (SRG/2020/001929) UGC for startup grant (30-561/2021 (BSR)).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125625.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

J. Colloid Interface Sci.

Zhao, A pH-dependent N, P co-doped carbon dots as fluorescent probe for malachite green assay and its visual application based on fluorescent hydrogel kit, Colloids and Surfaces B: Biointerfaces 221 (2023) 112985. Doi: 10.1016/j.colsurfb.2022.112985.
